deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this patient.
Let me go through the case step by step.
The patient is a 27-year-old man who's been having convulsive episodes.
He's had a history of seizures in the past, including a febrile seizure as a child and several concussions.
That makes me think about traumatic encephalopathy or something related to brain injury.
He's been taking levetiracetam and lamotrigine, but he stopped 3 days ago because he wasn't sure they were effective.
Then this morning, he had a sudden episode with a headache, blurred vision, numbness, and a convulsion.
During the episode, he was aware but had trouble hearing.
His MRI and CT scan were normal, so it's probably not a structural brain issue.
He has a history of burns and PTSD, which might contribute to his anxiety and stress levels.
His blood work shows slightly elevated lactate and CK, which could indicate some muscle activity or possible metabolic issues, but nothing too severe.
The fact that he stopped his meds and then had a seizure suggests that the epilepsy wasn't well-controlled.
The episodes are happening more frequently now.

The description of the convulsions, with the body stiffening, non-rhythmic tremors, and the response to external stimuli (like the physician's hand) makes me think of nonepileptic seizures or something like a dissociative state.
The patient also reported a history of trauma, which can lead to dissociative seizures.
The lack of incontinence and not biting his tongue doesn't fit with a typical tonic-clonic seizure.
Plus, he was able to follow commands after the episode, which is more in line with dissociative seizures where he's aware but not fully responsive.
So putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is dissociative seizures, possibly related to his past trauma and stress disorders.

deepseek-r1-distill-qwen-14b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 27-year-old man who's been having convulsions.
Let me go through the case step by step.
First, the patient had his first episode two years ago.
He experienced shaking of his trunk, arms, and legs, which lasted about 4-5 minutes.
Before that, he had a sharp pain in the back of his head, blurred vision, nausea, and other symptoms.
He didn't seek help, and it didn't happen again for 20 months.
Then, four months ago, while drinking alcohol, he had another episode with stiffness, shaking, and incontinence.
He went to the ER, had a normal CT scan and EEG, and started on levetiracetam.
But the seizures kept happening about once a week.
Three months ago, he saw a neurologist, had an MRI which was normal, and they added lamotrigine.
Still, the seizures continued.
One month ago, at work, he had more episodes.
Interestingly, when a doctor placed a hand on his shoulder during an episode, the shaking lessened, but resumed when the hand was removed.
He also showed Bell's phenomenon, where his eyes closed with upward deviation.
He could follow commands between episodes but had intermittent rocking and tremors.
On the day of admission, he stopped taking his meds three days prior.
He had a sharp head pain, blurred vision, numbness, and then a convulsion lasting up to 20 minutes.
He was aware during parts of it.
His history includes burns from a childhood fire, asthma, major depressive disorder, PTSD, panic disorder, and a family history of epilepsy and heart disease.
His mom has schizoaffective disorder and post-traumatic epilepsy.
He's been on levetiracetam and lamotrigine, but they didn't help.
He stopped them recently.
His blood work was normal, including glucose, electrolytes, lactate, and creatine kinase.
Toxicology was negative.
ECG was normal.
Now, thinking about possible causes.
The patient's seizures didn't respond to two different antiepileptic drugs, which is concerning.
The fact that the seizures worsened after stopping meds suggests they're real and not psychogenic.
The episodes have some unusual features: they can be modulated by touch (lessened when hand on shoulder), and he was partially aware during the episode.
The patient's history of burns and the extensive scarring makes me think about possible neurological issues from that.
He had a house fire as a child, which could have caused brain damage.
Also, his mother has post-traumatic epilepsy, which might suggest a genetic or trauma-related cause.
The fact that the EEG and MRI were normal is confusing because structural and electrical abnormalities are often seen in epilepsy.
But sometimes, especially in certain types of epilepsy, these tests can be normal.
The patient's ability to follow commands during the episode and the response to touch are interesting.
This might point towards a non-convulsive state or a different type of seizure.
Another possibility is that this isn't epilepsy at all.
The patient's history of trauma, burns, and psychological issues (PTSD, depression) makes me consider dissociative seizures or conversion disorder.
These are psychological in origin and can present with seizure-like activity.
They often don't show up on EEG or MRI and can be influenced by external factors, like touch.
The patient's improvement when touched and the presence of Bell's phenomenon might support a dissociative or psychological cause.
Also, the fact that he stopped his medications before the admission could have contributed to the increased frequency, but the initial episodes were also resistant to treatment.
Another angle is that the patient might have a non-epileptic seizure disorder, such as myoclonic seizures or something else.
But myoclonic seizures are usually brief and not associated with the described symptoms.

Putting it all together, the patient's history of trauma, lack of response to AEDs, and the nature of the seizures (with some responsiveness to touch and partial awareness) make me lean towards dissociative (psychogenic) non-epileptic seizures.
This is a condition where psychological stress manifests as physical symptoms, including seizures that don't have an organic cause.

huatuogpt-o1-8b.text: Okay, let's think through what's going on here.
We've got a 27-year-old guy who's having some pretty intense seizures.
They're happening out of nowhere, and it sounds like they last quite a while—like 4 to 5 minutes initially, and even longer in some cases.
That's definitely concerning.
Now, he's also experiencing some weird stuff right before these seizures start.
Like, he feels a sharp pain in his head, gets blurry vision, and has palpitations.
That's interesting.
It could be something related to his heart or maybe even a neurological issue.
He's tried different meds, like levetiracetam and lamotrigine, but they don't seem to be working.
That's frustrating.
And then there are these other things happening too.
He's got these 'rocking' movements and tremors when he's not seizing.
That's unusual.
Oh, and there's more.
During the seizures, he can still hear things sometimes, which is kind of strange.
Most people lose consciousness completely during a seizure.
Also, when someone touches him, the shaking stops temporarily.
That's a big clue.
Hmm, let's think about what could cause all this.
Could it be a type of epilepsy?
Maybe temporal lobe epilepsy?
But wait, those usually involve more focal symptoms, right?
This doesn't sound exactly like that.
What about psychogenic nonepileptic seizures?
Those often look like seizures but aren't caused by electrical activity in the brain.
People with PNES might have a psychological trigger, like trauma or stress.
This guy has PTSD and depression, so that fits.
But hold on, there's more.
He's got a history of concussions and a house fire.
That's a lot of trauma.
And his mom has post-traumatic epilepsy.
Could there be a genetic link?
Also, the EEG didn't show anything abnormal.
That's important.
If it was true epilepsy, we'd expect to see something on the EEG.
So, that makes me lean towards PNES again.
So, putting it all together, I'm thinking this is probably psychogenic nonepileptic seizures.
It seems like everything points to a psychological origin rather than a purely physical one.
Yeah, that makes sense given his history and the way the seizures behave.

qwq-32b.text: Okay, let's tackle this case.
The patient is a 27-year-old man with a history of convulsions.

The first thing I notice is the onset of symptoms two years prior, with episodes that include shaking of the trunk, arms, and legs, along with some prodromal symptoms like headache, blurred vision, palpitations, nausea, and difficulty concentrating.
The initial episode was followed by another after 20 months, which occurred during alcohol consumption.
The key here is that the initial EEG and CT were normal, but after starting levetiracetam, he still had weekly seizures.
Then lamotrigine was added without improvement.
The critical part comes from the description of the seizures at the second hospital.
During one episode, the shaking movements lessened when the physician touched his shoulder and resumed when the hand was removed.
Also, his eyes were closed, and when manually opened, there was no nystagmus or gaze deviation.
Bell's phenomenon was present when closing eyes.
He could follow commands during the episodes.
Between seizures, he had rocking and tremors but couldn't follow commands.
Wait, the fact that the movements stopped when touched and resumed when the touch was removed is really interesting.
That suggests a psychogenic component because psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) can have such triggers or responses to external stimuli.
Also, the ability to follow commands during the episode is more typical of PNES rather than true epileptic seizures, where consciousness is usually impaired.
The patient's history includes major depressive disorder, PTSD, and panic disorder, which are risk factors for PNES.

The family history mentions his mother has post-traumatic epilepsy, but the patient's own history includes a traumatic event (house fire leading to burns) and childhood maltreatment, which could contribute to psychological stress leading to PNES.
The EEGs were normal, but sometimes in PNES, EEG can be normal.
The MRI was also normal, which makes a structural lesion less likely.
The fact that he stopped his medications three days before the current admission might have led to a withdrawal seizure, but the description doesn't fit typical absence or tonic-clonic seizures.
The presence of awareness during the episodes (he was aware of events but had difficulty hearing) also points towards PNES.
The elevated creatine kinase could be due to muscle activity during the episodes, but not specific.
The lactate was slightly elevated, but not enough to suggest metabolic issues.
The normal glucose and electrolytes rule out some metabolic causes.

Putting it all together: the clinical features during the episodes (response to touch, preserved awareness, ability to follow commands), psychological history, and normal neuroimaging and EEG all point towards psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES).
The family history of epilepsy might have led to considering a genetic cause, but the presentation doesn't fit typical epilepsy given the normal studies and response to touch.
